source_texts: “That is not right, it’s a mistake!” Nina Alexandrovna intervened suddenly, looking almost ruefully at the Prince. “Mon mari se trompe.” *
“Thandavaraya, I don’t think this is a real horse. It may belong to the poi kaal kudirai (false horse performance) group,” said one of the soldiers. “Fine then.

(and Apollon Apollonovich could not form a clear and distinct idea of into what exactly). At any rate, Apollon Apollonovich would lose his new position: he could not accept it until he had washed away the disgraceful spots which had been made by his unworthy son and which had besmirched his honor.

“Ah!” he exclaimed, interrupting himself. “There come Mignon and Steiner! They are always together. You know that Steiner begins to have had enough of Rose; so the husband sticks to him like a plaster lest he should escape.”

“What? A priest? It’s not necessary. . . . Where would we get the money to pay him? I have no sins! Even without that God should forgive me. . . . He knows how I’ve suffered! And if He doesn’t forgive me, so be it!”

A thousand lords with girdle-jade walk in front and rear. The many flags of guardsmen stand both left and right. Those holding gilt bludgeons, And halberds and axes, March in pairs and pairs; The red silk lanterns, The royal incense urn, Move in solemnity. The dragons fly and the phoenixes dance; The falcons soar and the eagles take wing. This Son of Heaven’s an upright sage; The righteous ministers are good. They increase our bliss by a thousand years, surpassing Yu and Shun; They secure peace of ten thousand ages, rivaling Yao and Tang. We also see the curve-handled umbrella, And robes with rolling dragons— Their glare lighting up each other; The jade joined-rings, The phoenix fans, Waving through holy mist. Those caps of pearls and belts of jade; The purple sashes and medals of gold. A thousand rows of soldiers protect the Throne; Two lines of marshals uphold the carriage. This emperor, cleansed and sincere, bows to the Buddha, Glad to raise incense and seek virtue’s fruit. The grand cortege of the Tang emperor soon arrived in front of the temple. The emperor ordered a halt to the music, left the carriages, and led many officials in the worship of Buddha by taking up burning incense sticks in their hands. After bowing three times holding the incense, they raised their heads and looked around them. This was indeed a splendid religious hall. You see Dancing flags and banners; Bright, gleaming sunshades. Dancing flags and banners Fill the air with strands of flashing colored mists. Bright, gleaming sunshades Glow in the sun as fiery bolts. Imposing, the gold image of Lokājyeṣṭha;14 Most awesome, the jade features of the arhats. Divine flowers fill the vases. Sandalwood incense burn in the urns. The divine flowers filling the vases Adorn the temple with a brilliant forest of brocade. The sandalwood incense burning in the urns Covers the clear sky with waves of fragrant clouds. Piled high on red trays are fruits in season. On colored counters, mounds of cakes and sweets rest. Rows of noble priests chant the holy sūtras To save from their travails those orphaned souls. Taizong and his officials each lifted the incense; they also worshipped the golden body of the Buddha and paid homage to the arhats. Thereafter, the Master of the Law, Chen Xuanzang, the Grand Expositor of the Faith, led the various monks to greet the Tang emperor. After the ceremony, they went back to their seats according to their rank and station. The priest then presented Taizong with the proclamation for the deliverance of the orphaned souls. It read: The supreme virtue is vast and endless, for Buddhism is founded upon Nirvāṇa. The spirit of the pure and the clean circulates freely and flows everywhere in the Three Regions. There are a thousand changes and ten thousand transformations, all regulated by the forces of yin and yang. Boundless and vast indeed are the substance, the function, the true nature, and the permanence of such phenomena. But look at those orphaned souls, how worthy they are of our pity and commiseration! Now by the holy command of Taizong, we have selected and assembled various priests, who will engage in meditation and in the proclamation of the Law. Flinging wide the gates of salvation and setting in motion many vessels of mercy, we would deliver you, the multitudes, from the Sea of Woe and save you from perdition and from the Sixfold Path. You will be led to return to the way of truth and to enjoy the bliss of Heaven. Whether it be by motion, rest, or nonactivity, you will be united with, and become, pure essences. Therefore make use of this noble occasion, for you are invited to the pleasures of the celestial city. Take advantage of our Grand Mass so that you may find release from Hell’s confinement, ascend quickly and freely to ultimate bliss, and travel without restraint in the Region of the West.15 The poem says: An urn of immortal incense. Some scrolls of salvific power. As we proclaim this boundless Law, Receive now Heaven’s endless grace. All your guilt and crime abolished, You lost souls may leave your prison. May our nation be firmly blessed With peace long and all-embracing. Highly pleased by what he read, Taizong said to the monks, “Be firm, all of you, in your devotion, and do not slack in your service to Buddha. After the achievement of merit and after each has received his blessing, we shall reward you handsomely. Be assured that you will not have labored in vain.”

As for the man, he truly was so tired that he did not even make the most of those clean white sheets. He snuffed out his candle with his nostril, the way convicts do, and dropping on to the bed fully dressed, immediately fell into a deep sleep.

He followed the road-mender’s advice, went back, and half an hour later drove past the same spot but this time at a fast trot with a good trace horse. The horse-handler, who called himself a postilion, sat on the cart shaft.
‘When I wished to determine the increase in weight the Nautilus would require to dive, my only concern was the progressive reduction in volume that sea water experiences as it becomes deeper.’
From his corner in the back room of the Café Musain, more than drunk, Grantaire held forth in this manner, detaining the washer-up on her way through.
rationales: Outputs 0 and 4 share the same distinctive approach of translating the French phrase 'Mon mari se trompe' into English ('My husband is mistaken') rather than preserving the original French. Both also use double quotation marks, employ emotionally descriptive language for Nina Alexandrovna's expression ('disappointment and quiet resignation' vs. 'almost regretful expression'), and use similar verb choices ('interrupted'/'interjected abruptly'). All other outputs either preserve the French verbatim (1, 2) or omit the phrase entirely (3).

Outputs 3 and 4 share the same structural pattern: an exclamatory address to the soldier by name, followed by a two-sentence denial ('This is not a real horse') and identification of the false horse troupe, with both preserving the Tamil term 'poi kaal kudirai'/'Poi Kal Kudirai'. They use nearly identical sentence constructions and simple dialogue attribution ('one of the soldiers said'), distinguishing them from the other outputs which differ in tone, style, or added content.

Outputs 0 and 3 share the same structural and stylistic fingerprints: both open with Apollon Apollonovich's uncertainty framed as a parenthetical or near-parenthetical clause, both use clean modern prose without archaic vocabulary, both omit details like 'Ableukhov,' and both express the consequence (losing the position due to the son's shameful behavior) in similarly concise sentence structures. The word choices ('uncertain/unsure,' 'exact nature/situation,' 'shame/shameful,' 'couldn't take the position') are closely aligned.

Outputs 1 and 4 share several distinctive features: both use 'inseparable' to describe Mignon and Steiner, both employ modern colloquial language ('getting fed up'/'has started to distance himself'), and both follow a similar structural approach of cutting off and exclaiming with a modern, casual register. Their word choices and sentence constructions are stylistically closest to each other compared to the other outputs.

Outputs 2 and 4 share the most distinctive fingerprints: both use 'so be it' as the closing expression, both frame the money issue as a rhetorical question about affordability, both maintain a similar formal register and sentence structure, and both follow the same logical flow (priest rejection → money concern → no sins → God must forgive → suffering reference → 'so be it'). These stylistic and lexical choices distinguish them from the other outputs.

Outputs 2 and 4 share nearly identical structure, vocabulary, and content coverage. Both describe the grand procession with the same specific details (gilt bludgeons/halberds/axes, red silk lanterns, jade rings and phoenix fans, pearl caps/jade belts/purple sashes/gold medals, the Lokājyeṣṭha statue, sandalwood incense, red trays with seasonal fruits, the proclamation text, and the closing poem). The differences are minor rewordings (e.g., 'Sea of Suffering' vs 'Sea of Woe', 'curved-handled umbrellas' vs 'elegantly curved umbrellas'), which is consistent with the same paraphrasing system producing two slightly different outputs from the same source passage.

Outputs 0 and 3 share the same paraphraser. Both retain very close, archaic translation-style language: 'after the manner of convicts,' 'dressed as he was,' and the same sentence order (exhaustion noted first, then candle extinguishing, then falling on the bed). Both use 'clean/white sheets' and highly similar syntactic structures, distinguishing them from the more modernized or restructured outputs.

Outputs 0 and 4 share multiple distinctive linguistic fingerprints: both use 'road-mender's suggestion' (rather than 'advice'), both use 'Thirty minutes later' (rather than 'half an hour later'), both describe 'an additional horse pulling the cart', and both use 'cart shaft'/'cart's shaft' for the pole. These parallel word choices and structural similarities across multiple phrases strongly indicate they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 3 and 4 share nearly identical phrasing: both begin with 'When I wanted to calculate the increase in weight that the Nautilus would need to...' and both use the phrase 'progressive reduction in volume.' Their sentence structures and word choices are distinctly parallel compared to the other outputs, indicating they came from the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 1 and 2 share nearly identical stylistic fingerprints: both begin with 'Thus,' both use the phrase 'more than [drunk/intoxicated],' both reference 'catching' the dishwasher 'in her passage/on her way,' and both employ archaic, formal constructions that stay close to the source text's structure. The clause ordering and formal register are consistent between these two outputs.

human_par3: ‘That’s not true, it’s a mistake!’ Nina Alexandrovna said, turning to him suddenly, looking at him almost with anguish. ‘Mon mari se trompe. ’4
llama_31_8b: "There's a mistake here," Nina Alexandrovna interrupted firmly, her expression a mixture of disappointment and quiet resignation as she gazed at the Prince. "My husband is mistaken."
gemini_25_flash: "No, that's incorrect, it's an error!" Nina Alexandrovna interjected abruptly, glancing at the Prince with an almost regretful expression. "My husband is mistaken."
dipper_lowmed: “It’s not like that, it’s a mistake!” Nina Alexandrovna suddenly interjected, looking at the prince almost ruefully. “Mon mari se trompe.
Nina Alexandrovna suddenly intervened, looking almost sadly at the prince. “It’s not true, it’s a mistake!”

human_par3: “Thandavaraya, you'd better make sure this animal really is a horse, and not a false-legged prop, brought for the temple festivities,” mocked another soldier.
llama_31_8b: One of the soldiers expressed skepticism about the horse's authenticity, suggesting it might be part of a troupe that puts on fake horse performances.
gemini_25_flash: "Thandavaraya," a soldier remarked, "I doubt this is a genuine horse. It likely belongs to a folk performance troupe that uses prop horses." "Very well," he responded.
dipper_lowmed: “Thantavarayan! This is not a real horse. It is perhaps a false horse belonging to Poi Kal Kudirai,” said one of the soldiers.
“Thandavaraya! I don’t think this is a real horse. It seems to be a part of the poi kaal kudirai (false horse) troupe,” one of the soldiers said.

human_par3: This thought about ears slightly softened Apollon Apollonovich’s anger: Apollon Apollonovich postponed his intention of banishing his son from the house without initiating the most precise inquiry into the reasons that had led his son to wear the domino. But in any event Apollon Apollonovich would lose the post, he would have to decline the post; he could not accept the post without removing the shameful stains of his son’s behaviour (say what you will—an Ableukhov) that sullied the honour of the house.
llama_31_8b: Apollon Apollonovich couldn't pinpoint exactly what was bothering him, but he knew it had to do with the stain on his reputation. To take on the new position, he felt he first needed to clean up the tarnish left by his son's misdeeds, which had compromised his integrity.

Apollon Apollonovich was uncertain about what exactly the situation entailed. Nevertheless, he stood to lose his new position due to the shame cast upon him by his son's behavior, and he wouldn't be able to take on his role until he had somehow mitigated the stain on his honor.
gemini_25_flash: (Apollon Apollonovich was unsure of the exact nature of the situation). Regardless, Apollon Apollonovich's new role would be forfeited. He couldn't take the position until he had cleared his name of the shame brought upon it by his misbehaving son.
dipper_lowmed: At least, Apollon Apollonovich would lose his new position: he could not accept it until he had washed away the spots of disgrace made by his unworthy son and which stained his honor. (And Apollon Apollonovich could not clearly and precisely imagine what exactly this was.)

human_par3: "Tut!" he cried, breaking off short. " Mignon and Steiner. Always together. You know, Steiner's getting sick of Rose; that's why the husband dogs his steps now for fear of his slipping away."
llama_31_8b: He suddenly stopped speaking and exclaimed, "Here come Mignon and Steiner, always inseparable. You know, Steiner has started to distance himself from Rose, and now the husband is being extra clingy, glued to him like a stuck-on bandage, afraid he'll slip away."

He suddenly paused, exclaiming, "Oh, look, it's Mignon and Steiner approaching. Those two are always together. You know Steiner is growing tired of Rose, and now the husband is sticking close to him, almost as if holding him down, to prevent him from slipping away."
gemini_25_flash: "Oh!" he blurted out, cutting himself off. "Here come Mignon and Steiner! They're inseparable. You know Steiner is getting fed up with Rose, so her husband is clinging to him tightly to ensure he doesn't get away."
dipper_lowmed: "Ah!" he exclaimed, interrupting himself, "Here are Mignon and Steiner, always together. You know Steiner begins to have enough of Rose, and the husband sticks to him like a plaster, so as not to let him slip away."

human_par3: ‘What’s that? A priest?… Don’t bother… Where are you going to find a spare rouble? … I’ve no sins on me!… God will have to forgive me without that… He knows how I’ve suffered!… And if he won’t forgive me, too bad!…’
“What, the priest? I don’t want him. You haven’t got a ruble to spare. I have no sins. God must forgive me without that. He knows how I have suffered . . . And if He won’t forgive me, I don’t care!”
llama_31_8b: No need for a priest, we can't afford it anyway. I've done nothing wrong, and even if I had, I wouldn't need someone else to beg God for forgiveness. He knows all about the hardships I've endured, so if He can't find it in his heart to pardon me, I'll just have to accept it.
gemini_25_flash: "A priest? No need for that. How would we even afford one? I haven't committed any sins! God should just forgive me anyway; He's aware of my struggles. If not, then so be it."
dipper_lowmed: “What? A priest? It’s not necessary. And where could we find the money to pay him? I have no sins. Even without that God must forgive me. He knows how I have suffered! And if he doesn’t forgive me, so be it!...”

human_par3: There was no thunder and no lightning after this. This made the Wizard still more anxious, and he burnt more incense and charms and recited more incantations and struck the table as his fourth signal. Instantly the dragons from the four oceans appeared. Sun cried out above their heads, “Aoguang, where do you think you are going?” Aoguang and his companions came forward and bowed. Then Sun told them what he had told the other helpers before, and added, “You helped me before though not successfully. Today I hope you will help me with success.” The Dragon King replied, “I gladly obey your orders.” Sun said, “I beg of you to give me a helping hand. The Wizard’s signal of the fourth performance is now finished. It is now my turn, and I expect you all to help me. I burn no incense and have no signal but my club. When I lift up my spear the first time, I want the wind to blow.” The Mistress of the Wind said, “The wind shall blow.” “When I lift up my spear the second time, I want the clouds to gather.” The Master of the Clouds said, “The clouds shall gather.” “When I lift up my spear the third time, I want thunder and lightning.” The Master of Thunder and Mistress of Lightning said, “You shall have them in abundance.” “When I lift up my spear the fourth time, I want rain.” The Dragon replied, “Your orders shall be obeyed.” “When I lift up my spear the fifth time, I want a clear sky without fail.” Having arranged this, he restored the false Sun and took his place. He shouted out, “Master Wizard, your fourth signal for performance is now over and there has been neither wind, clouds, thunder, lightning, nor rain. Now it is my time to try.” The Wizard came down from his platform altar and went to the gallery by the king, and the Monkey followed him. The king asked the wizard how it was that none of his four attempts were successful. The wizard replied, “The rain king was not at home today.” The Monkey angrily replied, “The dragon gods were all at home, but your Royal Wizard is not sufficiently skilful.” The king then ordered the Buddhist monks to mount the platform. Sun invited the Master to go, but he said he could not command rain.
llama_31_8b:
A grand procession, led by nobles, marched past the imperial palace. Their banners and flags flapped in the wind, while guardsmen marched in pairs, their halberds and axes held high. The emperor, accompanied by his ministers, moved with dignified solemnity, followed by sunshades and parasols. Ash mappings, flapping and flying overhead, adding a whimsical touch to the scene. The dignitaries wore elaborate robes, intricately decorated with dragons and other motifs, and carried an array of sacred objects, including an umbrella, fans, and ritual instruments. As the entourage reached the temple, they halted the music and the emperor dismounted his carriage. He and his officials walked to the temple, taking care not to disturb the tranquil atmosphere within.

As they entered the grand hall, the emperor was struck by its beauty. The hall was adorned with colorful fabrics and flowers, the air was filled with the scent of incense, and the sound of chanting monks created a soothing melody. The emperor, accompanied by his officials, approached the Buddha image, bowed their heads in respect, and lit incense sticks. They then looked around, taking in the sights and sounds of the temple.

As the ceremony progressed, the monks, led by the renowned Master of the Law, Chen Xuanzang, approached the emperor. Taizong and his officials worshiped the Buddha and the arhats, and received sacred objects and offerings. After the ceremony, the monks returned to their seats, and Master Chen Xuanzang presented the emperor with a proclamation, a call to all to seek enlightenment. It spoke of the universal nature of all phenomena, the vastness of the human spirit, and the potential for each soul to reach Nirvāṇa. The emperor listened intently, moved by the words, which seemed to resonate deeply within him.
gemini_25_flash:
A grand procession of a thousand lords, adorned with jade, advanced with flags waving from guardsmen on all sides. An array of gilded bludgeons, halberds, and axes were carried by marching pairs. Red silk lanterns and the royal incense urn moved with stately dignity. Mythical creatures, dragons, and phoenixes, along with falcons and eagles, were depicted in flight, symbolizing the upright and wise emperor and his righteous ministers. This rule, it was proclaimed, would bring a thousand years of happiness, surpassing ancient legendary rulers Yu and Shun, and a peace lasting ten thousand ages, outshining Yao and Tang.

The procession also featured curved-handled umbrellas and robes embroidered with rolling dragons, their reflective surfaces casting a shimmering light. Jade rings and phoenix fans swayed through a sacred mist, while caps of pearls, jade belts, purple sashes, and gold medals adorned the participants. A thousand rows of soldiers flanked the Emperor’s carriage, protected by two lines of marshals. This pure and devoted emperor, seeking enlightenment, bowed to the Buddha, eager to offer incense and attain spiritual merit.

The Tang emperor's magnificent cortege soon reached the temple. The emperor commanded the music to cease, dismounted from his carriage, and, accompanied by many officials, worshipped the Buddha by holding burning incense sticks. After three bows with the incense, they looked around at the splendid religious hall.

Dancing flags and banners gleamed and swayed, filling the air with vibrant, flashing mist. Bright sunshades shimmered in the sun like fiery bolts. The golden statue of Lokājyeṣṭha was imposing, and the jade figures of the arhats were awe-inspiring. Vases overflowed with divine flowers, and sandalwood incense burned in urns. The divine flowers in the vases adorned the temple like a brilliant tapestry, while the burning sandalwood incense covered the clear sky with fragrant clouds. Red trays were piled high with seasonal fruits, and colorful counters displayed mounds of cakes and sweets. Rows of noble priests chanted sacred sūtras to rescue orphaned souls from suffering.

Taizong and his officials, after offering incense, also worshipped the golden Buddha statue and paid respects to the arhats. Following this, Chen Xuanzang, the Master of the Law and Grand Expositor of the Faith, led the monks to greet the Tang emperor. Once the ceremony concluded, everyone returned to their assigned seats. The priest then presented Taizong with a proclamation for the spiritual liberation of orphaned souls.

The proclamation read: "Virtue, boundless and infinite, underpins Buddhism, which is founded upon Nirvāṇa. The essence of purity and clarity pervades and flows throughout the Three Realms. Countless changes and transformations, all governed by the forces of yin and yang, are manifestations of its boundless and eternal substance, function, and true nature. We offer pity and compassion to those orphaned souls who suffer. By the sacred command of Taizong, we have gathered priests who will engage in meditation and proclaim the Law. By opening the gates of salvation and deploying vessels of mercy, we aim to deliver you, the multitudes, from the Sea of Suffering, saving you from perdition and the Sixfold Path. You will be guided back to the path of truth and experience the bliss of Heaven. Through motion, rest, or non-activity, you will unite with and become pure essences. Therefore, seize this noble opportunity, for you are invited to the joys of the celestial city. Take advantage of our Grand Mass to find release from Hell’s confines, ascend swiftly and freely to ultimate bliss, and journey unimpeded to the Western Region."

A poem concluded:
"An urn of immortal incense,
Some scrolls of saving power.
As we declare this boundless Law,
Receive now Heaven’s endless grace.
All your guilt and crimes abolished,
You lost souls may leave your prison.
May our nation be firmly blessed
With peace long and all-embracing."

Delighted by the proclamation, Taizong addressed the monks, saying, "All of you, remain steadfast in your devotion and do not falter in your service to Buddha. After achieving merit and receiving your blessings, we will reward you generously. Be assured that your efforts will not be in vain."

A grand procession led by a multitude of officials, adorned with elaborate jade and silk, marched in perfect formation. Guardsmen, bearing an array of weapons and royal symbols like gilt bludgeons, halberds, and axes, marched in pairs. Red silk lanterns and the royal incense urn moved with solemnity. Mythical creatures like dragons and phoenixes were depicted as soaring and dancing, while real falcons and eagles took flight. This demonstrated the emperor, described as a virtuous and wise ruler, and his righteous ministers, who were credited with bringing immense prosperity and enduring peace, surpassing even legendary past leaders. The parade also featured elegantly curved umbrellas and robes with intricate dragon designs that shimmered, alongside jade rings and phoenix fans that waved through a sacred mist. Officials wore pearl-studded caps, jade belts, purple sashes, and gold medals. A thousand soldiers guarded the imperial throne, and two lines of marshals flanked the emperor's carriage.

The emperor, portraying himself as a cleansed and devout figure, bowed to the Buddha, eager to offer incense and seek spiritual rewards. The Tang emperor's magnificent procession soon arrived at the temple. He commanded the music to cease, exited his carriage, and, accompanied by many officials, participated in the Buddha worship by lighting incense sticks. After bowing three times with the incense, they looked up, observing the splendor of the religious hall.

Flags and banners fluttered, creating a shimmering, colorful haze, while bright, gleaming sunshades glowed like fiery bolts in the sun. Inside, a grand golden statue of Lokājyeṣṭha and imposing jade figures of arhats commanded attention. Vases held divine flowers, filling the temple with the vibrant appearance of a brocade forest, and sandalwood incense burned in urns, its fragrant smoke forming clouds that drifted across the clear sky. Red trays were stacked high with seasonal fruits, and colorful counters displayed mounds of cakes and sweets. Rows of noble priests chanted sacred sūtras, aiming to deliver orphaned souls from suffering.

Taizong and his officials, after offering incense, also paid homage to the golden Buddha and the arhats. Following this, Chen Xuanzang, the Grand Expositor of the Faith and Master of the Law, led the monks in greeting the Tang emperor. Once the ceremony concluded, everyone returned to their designated places according to their rank. The priest then presented Taizong with a proclamation for the deliverance of orphaned souls.

The proclamation stated that the supreme virtue of Buddhism, rooted in Nirvāṇa, is vast and limitless. The pure and clean spirit flows freely throughout the Three Regions, with countless transformations governed by yin and yang. It emphasized the boundless nature of such phenomena, while lamenting the pitiable state of orphaned souls. By Taizong's holy command, priests had been assembled to meditate and proclaim the Law. They aimed to open the gates of salvation and use vessels of mercy to rescue the multitudes from the Sea of Woe, saving them from perdition and the Sixfold Path. The goal was to guide them back to truth and divine bliss, uniting them with pure essences through motion, rest, or nonactivity. The proclamation invited everyone to embrace this noble opportunity, to enjoy the celestial city, and through the Grand Mass, to find release from Hell, ascend to ultimate bliss, and freely journey to the Western Region.

A poem reinforced this message: an urn of immortal incense and salvific scrolls proclaiming boundless Law offered endless divine grace. All guilt and crime would be abolished, freeing lost souls from their prison, and blessing the nation with lasting peace.

Taizong, highly pleased by the text, instructed the monks to remain steadfast in their devotion and diligence in serving the Buddha. He promised handsome rewards after they achieved merit and received their blessings, assuring them their efforts would not be in vain.

llama_31_8b: The man was so exhausted that the comfort of the fresh white sheets meant little to him. He extinguished his candle by blowing it out through his nose, a behavior often associated with prisoners, and, still fully dressed, collapsed into a deep slumber as soon as he lay down on the bed.
gemini_25_flash: The man was so utterly exhausted that he barely registered the fresh, clean sheets on the bed. Like a prisoner, he extinguished his candle by pinching it with his nostril, then collapsed onto the bed fully clothed, instantly falling into a profound slumber.

human_par3: Thus did Grantaire, more than intoxicated, launch into speech, catching at the dish-washer in her passage, from his corner in the back room of the Café Musain.
Thus Grantaire, more than drunk, spread himself out in words catching up the dishwasher on her way, in his corner of the Musain back room.
llama_31_8b: Slumped against the back wall of the Café Musain, Grantaire slurred away at the kitchen staff, blocking a server's path in her haste to clean.
gemini_25_flash: Grantaire, quite intoxicated, spoke at length from his spot in the rear of the Café Musain, waylaying the dishwasher as she passed by.
dipper_lowmed: And Grantaire, who was more than drunk, continued in this way from his corner in the back of the Café Musain, stopping the laundress as she was going past.

responses: "There's a mistake here," Nina Alexandrovna interrupted firmly, her expression a mixture of disappointment and quiet resignation as she gazed at the Prince. "My husband is mistaken."
One of the soldiers expressed skepticism about the horse's authenticity, suggesting it might be part of a troupe that puts on fake horse performances.

Apollon Apollonovich was uncertain about what exactly the situation entailed. Nevertheless, he stood to lose his new position due to the shame cast upon him by his son's behavior, and he wouldn't be able to take on his role until he had somehow mitigated the stain on his honor.

"Ah!" he exclaimed, interrupting himself, "Here are Mignon and Steiner, always together. You know Steiner begins to have enough of Rose, and the husband sticks to him like a plaster, so as not to let him slip away."
“What, the priest? I don’t want him. You haven’t got a ruble to spare. I have no sins. God must forgive me without that. He knows how I have suffered . . . And if He won’t forgive me, I don’t care!”

These fine gifts from the Throne, The many flags of the guards, All enhance our renown to surpass that of Yu and Shun; These righteous deeds from ministers, Equal that of Yao and Tang. The dragons soar, the phoenixes soar, The falcons soar, the eagles soar; The Son of Heaven is a true sage, His ministers are righteous men.

As for the man, he was actually so fatigued that he did not even profit by the nice white sheets. Snuffing out his candle with his nostrils after the manner of convicts, he dropped, all dressed as he was, upon the bed, where he immediately fell into a profound sleep.

Taking the road-mender's suggestion, he returned to the location. Thirty minutes later, he was driving past the same spot again, but this time much more quickly, thanks to an additional horse pulling the cart. A person who identified as a postilion was perched on the cart's shaft, guiding the horses.
As I calculated the necessary increase in weight for the Nautilus to dive, my main consideration was the decrease in water volume that occurs with increasing depth.
Slumped against the back wall of the Café Musain, Grantaire slurred away at the kitchen staff, blocking a server's path in her haste to clean.
‘That’s not true, it’s a mistake!’ Nina Alexandrovna said, turning to him suddenly, looking at him almost with anguish. ‘Mon mari se trompe. ’4
"Thandavaraya," a soldier remarked, "I doubt this is a genuine horse. It likely belongs to a folk performance troupe that uses prop horses." "Very well," he responded.

This thought about ears slightly softened Apollon Apollonovich’s anger: Apollon Apollonovich postponed his intention of banishing his son from the house without initiating the most precise inquiry into the reasons that had led his son to wear the domino. But in any event Apollon Apollonovich would lose the post, he would have to decline the post; he could not accept the post without removing the shameful stains of his son’s behaviour (say what you will—an Ableukhov) that sullied the honour of the house.

He suddenly stopped speaking and exclaimed, "Here come Mignon and Steiner, always inseparable. You know, Steiner has started to distance himself from Rose, and now the husband is being extra clingy, glued to him like a stuck-on bandage, afraid he'll slip away."

No need for a priest, we can't afford it anyway. I've done nothing wrong, and even if I had, I wouldn't need someone else to beg God for forgiveness. He knows all about the hardships I've endured, so if He can't find it in his heart to pardon me, I'll just have to accept it.

There was no thunder and no lightning after this. This made the Wizard still more anxious, and he burnt more incense and charms and recited more incantations and struck the table as his fourth signal. Instantly the dragons from the four oceans appeared. Sun cried out above their heads, “Aoguang, where do you think you are going?” Aoguang and his companions came forward and bowed. Then Sun told them what he had told the other helpers before, and added, “You helped me before though not successfully. Today I hope you will help me with success.” The Dragon King replied, “I gladly obey your orders.” Sun said, “I beg of you to give me a helping hand. The Wizard’s signal of the fourth performance is now finished. It is now my turn, and I expect you all to help me. I burn no incense and have no signal but my club. When I lift up my spear the first time, I want the wind to blow.” The Mistress of the Wind said, “The wind shall blow.” “When I lift up my spear the second time, I want the clouds to gather.” The Master of the Clouds said, “The clouds shall gather.” “When I lift up my spear the third time, I want thunder and lightning.” The Master of Thunder and Mistress of Lightning said, “You shall have them in abundance.” “When I lift up my spear the fourth time, I want rain.” The Dragon replied, “Your orders shall be obeyed.” “When I lift up my spear the fifth time, I want a clear sky without fail.” Having arranged this, he restored the false Sun and took his place. He shouted out, “Master Wizard, your fourth signal for performance is now over and there has been neither wind, clouds, thunder, lightning, nor rain. Now it is my time to try.” The Wizard came down from his platform altar and went to the gallery by the king, and the Monkey followed him. The king asked the wizard how it was that none of his four attempts were successful. The wizard replied, “The rain king was not at home today.” The Monkey angrily replied, “The dragon gods were all at home, but your Royal Wizard is not sufficiently skilful.” The king then ordered the Buddhist monks to mount the platform. Sun invited the Master to go, but he said he could not command rain.

The man was so utterly exhausted that he barely registered the fresh, clean sheets on the bed. Like a prisoner, he extinguished his candle by pinching it with his nostril, then collapsed onto the bed fully clothed, instantly falling into a profound slumber.

After being advised to turn back, he headed in the opposite direction, and half an hour later he passed the same location again, but this time at a quick pace with a strong trace horse in harness. The man handling the horse, who called himself a postilion, sat on the cart pole.

“It’s not like that, it’s a mistake!” Nina Alexandrovna suddenly interjected, looking at the prince almost ruefully. “Mon mari se trompe.
“Thandavaraya, you'd better make sure this animal really is a horse, and not a false-legged prop, brought for the temple festivities,” mocked another soldier.

Apollon Apollonovich couldn't pinpoint exactly what was bothering him, but he knew it had to do with the stain on his reputation. To take on the new position, he felt he first needed to clean up the tarnish left by his son's misdeeds, which had compromised his integrity.
"Tut!" he cried, breaking off short. " Mignon and Steiner. Always together. You know, Steiner's getting sick of Rose; that's why the husband dogs his steps now for fear of his slipping away."
"A priest? No need for that. How would we even afford one? I haven't committed any sins! God should just forgive me anyway; He's aware of my struggles. If not, then so be it."

A grand procession of a thousand lords, adorned with jade, advanced with flags waving from guardsmen on all sides. An array of gilded bludgeons, halberds, and axes were carried by marching pairs. Red silk lanterns and the royal incense urn moved with stately dignity. Mythical creatures, dragons, and phoenixes, along with falcons and eagles, were depicted in flight, symbolizing the upright and wise emperor and his righteous ministers. This rule, it was proclaimed, would bring a thousand years of happiness, surpassing ancient legendary rulers Yu and Shun, and a peace lasting ten thousand ages, outshining Yao and Tang.

The procession also featured curved-handled umbrellas and robes embroidered with rolling dragons, their reflective surfaces casting a shimmering light. Jade rings and phoenix fans swayed through a sacred mist, while caps of pearls, jade belts, purple sashes, and gold medals adorned the participants. A thousand rows of soldiers flanked the Emperor’s carriage, protected by two lines of marshals. This pure and devoted emperor, seeking enlightenment, bowed to the Buddha, eager to offer incense and attain spiritual merit.

The Tang emperor's magnificent cortege soon reached the temple. The emperor commanded the music to cease, dismounted from his carriage, and, accompanied by many officials, worshipped the Buddha by holding burning incense sticks. After three bows with the incense, they looked around at the splendid religious hall.

Dancing flags and banners gleamed and swayed, filling the air with vibrant, flashing mist. Bright sunshades shimmered in the sun like fiery bolts. The golden statue of Lokājyeṣṭha was imposing, and the jade figures of the arhats were awe-inspiring. Vases overflowed with divine flowers, and sandalwood incense burned in urns. The divine flowers in the vases adorned the temple like a brilliant tapestry, while the burning sandalwood incense covered the clear sky with fragrant clouds. Red trays were piled high with seasonal fruits, and colorful counters displayed mounds of cakes and sweets. Rows of noble priests chanted sacred sūtras to rescue orphaned souls from suffering.

Taizong and his officials, after offering incense, also worshipped the golden Buddha statue and paid respects to the arhats. Following this, Chen Xuanzang, the Master of the Law and Grand Expositor of the Faith, led the monks to greet the Tang emperor. Once the ceremony concluded, everyone returned to their assigned seats. The priest then presented Taizong with a proclamation for the spiritual liberation of orphaned souls.

The proclamation read: "Virtue, boundless and infinite, underpins Buddhism, which is founded upon Nirvāṇa. The essence of purity and clarity pervades and flows throughout the Three Realms. Countless changes and transformations, all governed by the forces of yin and yang, are manifestations of its boundless and eternal substance, function, and true nature. We offer pity and compassion to those orphaned souls who suffer. By the sacred command of Taizong, we have gathered priests who will engage in meditation and proclaim the Law. By opening the gates of salvation and deploying vessels of mercy, we aim to deliver you, the multitudes, from the Sea of Suffering, saving you from perdition and the Sixfold Path. You will be guided back to the path of truth and experience the bliss of Heaven. Through motion, rest, or non-activity, you will unite with and become pure essences. Therefore, seize this noble opportunity, for you are invited to the joys of the celestial city. Take advantage of our Grand Mass to find release from Hell’s confines, ascend swiftly and freely to ultimate bliss, and journey unimpeded to the Western Region."

A poem concluded:
"An urn of immortal incense,
Some scrolls of saving power.
As we declare this boundless Law,
Receive now Heaven’s endless grace.
All your guilt and crimes abolished,
You lost souls may leave your prison.
May our nation be firmly blessed
With peace long and all-embracing."

Delighted by the proclamation, Taizong addressed the monks, saying, "All of you, remain steadfast in your devotion and do not falter in your service to Buddha. After achieving merit and receiving your blessings, we will reward you generously. Be assured that your efforts will not be in vain."

The man was so exhausted that the comfort of the fresh white sheets meant little to him. He extinguished his candle by blowing it out through his nose, a behavior often associated with prisoners, and, still fully dressed, collapsed into a deep slumber as soon as he lay down on the bed.

The yokel, who called himself the postilion, sat on the shaft. He followed the advice of the road-mender, went back and, half an hour later, passed the same place, but this time at a fast trot, with a good trace horse.
When I wanted to find out the necessary increase of weight required to sink the Nautilus, I had only to calculate the reduction of volume that sea-water acquires according to the depth.”

Nina Alexandrovna suddenly intervened, looking almost sadly at the prince. “It’s not true, it’s a mistake!”
“Thandavaraya! I don’t think this is a real horse. It seems to be a part of the poi kaal kudirai (false horse) troupe,” one of the soldiers said.

He suddenly paused, exclaiming, "Oh, look, it's Mignon and Steiner approaching. Those two are always together. You know Steiner is growing tired of Rose, and now the husband is sticking close to him, almost as if holding him down, to prevent him from slipping away."

‘What’s that? A priest?… Don’t bother… Where are you going to find a spare rouble? … I’ve no sins on me!… God will have to forgive me without that… He knows how I’ve suffered!… And if he won’t forgive me, too bad!…’

A grand procession, led by nobles, marched past the imperial palace. Their banners and flags flapped in the wind, while guardsmen marched in pairs, their halberds and axes held high. The emperor, accompanied by his ministers, moved with dignified solemnity, followed by sunshades and parasols. Ash mappings, flapping and flying overhead, adding a whimsical touch to the scene. The dignitaries wore elaborate robes, intricately decorated with dragons and other motifs, and carried an array of sacred objects, including an umbrella, fans, and ritual instruments. As the entourage reached the temple, they halted the music and the emperor dismounted his carriage. He and his officials walked to the temple, taking care not to disturb the tranquil atmosphere within.

As they entered the grand hall, the emperor was struck by its beauty. The hall was adorned with colorful fabrics and flowers, the air was filled with the scent of incense, and the sound of chanting monks created a soothing melody. The emperor, accompanied by his officials, approached the Buddha image, bowed their heads in respect, and lit incense sticks. They then looked around, taking in the sights and sounds of the temple.

As the ceremony progressed, the monks, led by the renowned Master of the Law, Chen Xuanzang, approached the emperor. Taizong and his officials worshiped the Buddha and the arhats, and received sacred objects and offerings. After the ceremony, the monks returned to their seats, and Master Chen Xuanzang presented the emperor with a proclamation, a call to all to seek enlightenment. It spoke of the universal nature of all phenomena, the vastness of the human spirit, and the potential for each soul to reach Nirvāṇa. The emperor listened intently, moved by the words, which seemed to resonate deeply within him.

As to the man, he was so completely exhausted that he did not even avail himself of the clean white sheets; he blew out the candle with his nostril, after the manner of convicts, and fell on the bed, dressed as he was, into a sound sleep.

He followed the road-mender’s advice, retraced his steps, and, half an hour later, he passed the same spot again, but this time at full speed, with a good horse to aid; a stable-boy, who called himself a postilion, was seated on the shaft of the cariole.

"Oh!" he blurted out, cutting himself off. "Here come Mignon and Steiner! They're inseparable. You know Steiner is getting fed up with Rose, so her husband is clinging to him tightly to ensure he doesn't get away."

“What? A priest? It’s not necessary. And where could we find the money to pay him? I have no sins. Even without that God must forgive me. He knows how I have suffered! And if he doesn’t forgive me, so be it!...”

A grand procession led by a multitude of officials, adorned with elaborate jade and silk, marched in perfect formation. Guardsmen, bearing an array of weapons and royal symbols like gilt bludgeons, halberds, and axes, marched in pairs. Red silk lanterns and the royal incense urn moved with solemnity. Mythical creatures like dragons and phoenixes were depicted as soaring and dancing, while real falcons and eagles took flight. This demonstrated the emperor, described as a virtuous and wise ruler, and his righteous ministers, who were credited with bringing immense prosperity and enduring peace, surpassing even legendary past leaders. The parade also featured elegantly curved umbrellas and robes with intricate dragon designs that shimmered, alongside jade rings and phoenix fans that waved through a sacred mist. Officials wore pearl-studded caps, jade belts, purple sashes, and gold medals. A thousand soldiers guarded the imperial throne, and two lines of marshals flanked the emperor's carriage.

The emperor, portraying himself as a cleansed and devout figure, bowed to the Buddha, eager to offer incense and seek spiritual rewards. The Tang emperor's magnificent procession soon arrived at the temple. He commanded the music to cease, exited his carriage, and, accompanied by many officials, participated in the Buddha worship by lighting incense sticks. After bowing three times with the incense, they looked up, observing the splendor of the religious hall.

Flags and banners fluttered, creating a shimmering, colorful haze, while bright, gleaming sunshades glowed like fiery bolts in the sun. Inside, a grand golden statue of Lokājyeṣṭha and imposing jade figures of arhats commanded attention. Vases held divine flowers, filling the temple with the vibrant appearance of a brocade forest, and sandalwood incense burned in urns, its fragrant smoke forming clouds that drifted across the clear sky. Red trays were stacked high with seasonal fruits, and colorful counters displayed mounds of cakes and sweets. Rows of noble priests chanted sacred sūtras, aiming to deliver orphaned souls from suffering.

Taizong and his officials, after offering incense, also paid homage to the golden Buddha and the arhats. Following this, Chen Xuanzang, the Grand Expositor of the Faith and Master of the Law, led the monks in greeting the Tang emperor. Once the ceremony concluded, everyone returned to their designated places according to their rank. The priest then presented Taizong with a proclamation for the deliverance of orphaned souls.

The proclamation stated that the supreme virtue of Buddhism, rooted in Nirvāṇa, is vast and limitless. The pure and clean spirit flows freely throughout the Three Regions, with countless transformations governed by yin and yang. It emphasized the boundless nature of such phenomena, while lamenting the pitiable state of orphaned souls. By Taizong's holy command, priests had been assembled to meditate and proclaim the Law. They aimed to open the gates of salvation and use vessels of mercy to rescue the multitudes from the Sea of Woe, saving them from perdition and the Sixfold Path. The goal was to guide them back to truth and divine bliss, uniting them with pure essences through motion, rest, or nonactivity. The proclamation invited everyone to embrace this noble opportunity, to enjoy the celestial city, and through the Grand Mass, to find release from Hell, ascend to ultimate bliss, and freely journey to the Western Region.

A poem reinforced this message: an urn of immortal incense and salvific scrolls proclaiming boundless Law offered endless divine grace. All guilt and crime would be abolished, freeing lost souls from their prison, and blessing the nation with lasting peace.

Taizong, highly pleased by the text, instructed the monks to remain steadfast in their devotion and diligence in serving the Buddha. He promised handsome rewards after they achieved merit and received their blessings, assuring them their efforts would not be in vain.

He snuffed out his candle with his nostrils, as prisoners do, and threw himself on the bed fully dressed, and immediately fell into a deep sleep. As for the man, he was so tired that he did not even take advantage of these clean white sheets.
